title: High CPU Investigation
scenario: The on-call engineer has been paged: a server is running at near 100% CPU but the monitoring dashboard shows no known scheduled job is active. You have been asked to SSH in, identify the runaway process using real-time CPU tools, record its PID for the incident report, and kill it to restore normal operation.
symptoms: Server CPU is pegged at or near 100%
The offending process is not immediately obvious from the process name alone
Normal workloads are degraded due to resource starvation
mission: Use 'top' (sorted by CPU) or 'htop' to identify the process consuming the most CPU. Write its PID to /home/intern/culprit_pid.txt, then kill the process to restore normal CPU usage.

hints.title: Launch top and sort by CPU
hints.content: Run 'top'. The process list is already sorted by CPU usage by default — the highest consumer appears at the top. Note the PID in the first column. Press 'q' to quit.

hints.title: Try htop for a clearer view
hints.content: Run 'htop'. It shows a colour bar graph of CPU usage per core at the top and sorts processes by CPU automatically. The PID column is on the left. Press F10 or 'q' to exit.

hints.title: Record the PID
hints.content: Write the PID to /home/intern/culprit_pid.txt. For example: echo 1234 > /home/intern/culprit_pid.txt

hints.title: Kill the process
hints.content: Use 'kill <PID>' to send SIGTERM to the process. Verify it is gone with 'pgrep yes' — it should return nothing.

acceptance_criteria.description: culprit_pid.txt contains the correct yes process PID
yes process has been killed (no longer running)
